{{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} |ISO = JP-11 |rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} |ISO = JP-11 |rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} |ISO = JP-11 |rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * ([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} |ISO = JP-11 |rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} |ISO = JP-11 |rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * Inari Shrine] (official site) * {{Twitter|yakyuinari0921}} * {{Instagram|yakyuinari}} * {{Facebook|Yakyu Inari Shrine-683229961783950}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} |ISO = JP-11 |rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道}} == References == {{Reflist}}
