([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=draft}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=draft}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|12=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|12=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|12=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|12=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|12=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|12=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|12=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|12=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|12=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|ja|神社一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|500 Beautiful Japanese Roads You Want to Walk|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.yakyu-inari.jp/ Yakyu Inari Shrine] (official site) * {{Twitter|yakyuinari0921}} * {{Instagram|yakyuinari}} * {{Facebook|Yakyu Inari Shrine-683229961783950}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]s}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{citation needed|Yakyu Inari Shrine}} {{citation needed|Yakyu Inari Shrine}} {{citation needed|Yakyu Inari Shrine}} {{citation needed|Yakyu Inari Shrine}} {{citation needed|Yakyu Inari Shrine}} The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|12=draft|12=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|12=draft|12=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|12=draft|12=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=draft|12=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|12=draft|12=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|12=draft|12=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|12=draft|12=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|ja|神社一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|500 Beautiful Japanese Roads You Want to Walk|ja|美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.yakyu-inari.jp/ Yakyu Inari Shrine] (official site) * {{Twitter|yakyuinari0921}} * {{Instagram|yakyuinari}} * {{Facebook|Yakyu Inari Shrine-683229961783950}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|12=draft|12=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|12=draft|12=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|12=draft|12=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=draft|12=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|12=draft|12=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|12=draft|12=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|12=draft|12=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=draft|12=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|12=draft|12=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|12=draft|12=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|12=draft|12=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * ([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}}. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|12=draft|12=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|12=draft|12=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=draft|12=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|12=draft|12=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|12=draft|12=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|12=draft|12=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}} |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=draft|12=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft|12=draft}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|12=draft|12=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|12=draft|12=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|12=draft|12=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|12=draft|12=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|12=draft|12=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft|12=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=draft|12=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|12=draft|12=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|12=draft|12=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|12=draft|12=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|12=draft|12=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|12=draft|12=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|12=draft|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=draft|12=draft}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite revision.text:
{{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District 10|chapter=Matsuyama Town|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=Geography Bureau, Ministry of Home Affairs|date=June 1884|ref={{sfnref|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript Matsuyama Town|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of revision.comment: 82 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市) up to rev 102322680
revision.text:
{{redirect|箭弓稲荷神社|[[東京都]][[台東区]]にある当社の[[分霊]]である箭弓稲荷神社|箭弓稲荷神社 (台東区)}} {{参照方法|date=2013年8月}} {{神社 |名称=箭弓稲荷神社 |画像=[[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居と拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |所在地=[[埼玉県]][[東松山市]]箭弓町2-5-14 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 36 |緯度分 = 02 |緯度秒 = 04 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 23 |経度秒 = 55 |祭神=[[保食神]]
([[宇迦之御魂神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受比賣神]]) |社格=旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建=[[712年]]（和銅5年） |本殿=[[権現造]] |別名=やきゅうさま |例祭=[[9月21日]] |神事= |地図 = Japan Saitama }} '''箭弓稲荷神社'''（やきゅういなりじんじゃ）とは、[[埼玉県]][[東松山市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は県社で、戦後は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]となった。 まれに[[稲荷神#日本三大稲荷|日本三大稲荷]]{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11.箭弓稲荷神社 |newspaper=足立朝日 |publisher=朝日新聞販売店 足立朝日会 |date=2009-11-20 |accessdate=2018-11-16}}{{要出典|date=2018年12月|title=箭弓稲荷神社自体に対し日本三大稲荷の一つである案内とする文献}}や[[稲荷神#日本五大稲荷|日本五大稲荷]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=西口店近くの有名な神社です 箭弓稲荷神社 |publisher=[[ピタットハウス]]東松山西口店 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{要出典|date=2018年12月|title=箭弓稲荷神社が日本五大稲荷の一つであるとする文献}}の一つとされることがある。 ==歴史== [[712年]]（和銅5年）の創建と伝えられる。その頃は小さな祠だったようだが、[[1030年]]（長元3年）に[[下総国]]の城主[[平忠常]]の討伐に出かけた[[源頼信]]がこの周辺に一泊した際に、近くにあった野久稲荷神社に詣でて、太刀一振と馬一頭を奉納すると、その夜に白羽の矢のような形をした雲が敵陣の方へ飛んでいくのを目撃し、これは神のお告げだと確信、直ちに敵陣に攻め込んだ頼信は快勝し、立派な社殿を建造し「箭弓稲荷大明神」とたたえたと伝えられる。以来、[[松山城 (武蔵国)|松山城]]主、[[川越城]]主などの庇護を受けた。 社名の由来については、[[1028年]]（長元元年）に[[下総国]]の[[平忠常]]が謀反を企て[[安房国|安房]]・[[上総国|上総]]・[[下総国|下総]]の三カ国を制圧し、大軍をもって[[武蔵国]]に侵攻した（[[平忠常の乱]]）。これを受けて源氏の棟梁[[源満仲]]（多田満仲）の子であり、[[甲斐国]]守を勤める[[源頼信]]は、忠常追討の綸旨を賜り、鎮圧に向かった。しかし、多勢に無勢、武勇の頼信も心を悩ませた。その時、頼信が布陣する松山本陣近くで古い祠を見つけた。問えば、野久原に鎮まる「野久稲荷大明神」で、本地は「[[十一面観音|十一面観世音]]」であるという。 これを聞いた頼信は、野久はすなわち箭弓（矢弓）の意で、武門の守護神であると、大いに奮い立ち、神前に怨敵退散の願書、太刀一振、駿馬一頭を奉納し、ついに忠常を撃退することができた。一説には、白狐に乗った神が弓矢を授けたと伝える。この神恩に報いるため、社殿を再建した。以来、野久稲荷は、箭弓稲荷と号せられるようになった。 同形の物語としては、さかのぼること[[791年]]（延暦10年）[[坂上田村麻呂]]が箭弓稲荷より南西約4kmにある[[正法寺 (東松山市)|岩殿観音]]付近の雪見峠（セメント採掘により峠としては現存しないが、地名として残っており現在の東松山市大字岩殿字雪見峠付近）の悪竜を退治する際に、老人の姿の神が矢を授けたとの言い伝えがある。 一方で関連する寺院として、福聚寺の草創については次のようにある。当社は中世の兵乱により衰微し、野中の小社となり、近くの庵主が神前に一灯を供ずるのみになっていた。[[1617年]]（元和3年）、[[天海|天海僧正]]が[[駿府]]から[[下野国]]へ神輿（二度葬される途中の家康と思われる）を守護し、当地松山宿を通行した折、大雨に見舞われたため、当社の宮守りをしていた庵主の草室に神輿を納めた。すると、忽然と弓箭を携えた翁が示現した。天海僧正が何者かと問えば「人にあらず、稲魂の神使なり、僧正守護する神輿の御先を掃仕して非常を静める役を、この松山野久の地に勤めん」と告げた。これを聞いた僧正は、庵主に翁の御告を伝えて当社の由来を聞き、箭弓稲荷大神を尊崇して社殿を造営した。この折、僧正は訪れた庵を一寺に取り立てて福聚寺という寺号を与え、庵主を別当職に補任した。 『[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]』には「享保中より殊に威徳著しく諸人信仰するもの多し　今の如く市店旅宿門前に並へるは彼頃よりのことなり」とある{{sfn|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}。 [[明治]]初年、[[神仏分離]]により福聚寺は別当から離れることとなった。（福聚寺は今も東松山市本町に存在している（[[東松山市立図書館|市立図書館]]から沼をはさんだ反対側）） ==祭神== *[[保食神]]（宇迦之御魂神・豊受比賣神） ==境内== かつては広大な敷地を持っていたが、駅前に位置している事もあり、[[ホテル]]や[[自動車学校]]、テニスコートなどに代わってしまっている。 *'''本殿'''－[[1715年]]（正徳5年）に地頭・島田弾正の計らいにより建造されたもの。 *'''幣殿'''－[[1811年]]（文化8年）の建造と伝えられる。 *'''拝殿'''－[[1835年]]（天保6年）に領主 松平大和守の造営。 *'''穴宮稲荷（団十郎稲荷）'''　- [[市川團十郎_(7代目)|7代目市川団十郎]]は、芸道一筋に生きた歌舞伎役者で当社の熱心な崇敬者であったと伝える。社伝によると、歌舞伎の演じ物に「[[葛の葉]]」「[[義経千本桜|狐忠信]](義経千本桜四段目 河連法眼館の段)」があり、狐の仕草が非常に難しいとされるが、団十郎は稲荷大神に心願し、その加護を受け、ついに江戸柳盛座で見事に演じきり、大盛況のうちに興行を終えることができたという。そのため、大願成就のための石祠一社を[[1821年]]（文政4年）に奉納している{{sfn|清水宣弘|1981|pp=54-59}}。これは、お穴様と呼ばれる穴宮神社で、現在でも技芸向上に励む人々から信仰されている。 ==文化財== * 本殿・幣殿・拝殿（附 本宮本殿1棟、棟札3枚、文書1冊）（建造物） - 国の[[重要文化財]][https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html 文化審議会の答申（重要文化財（建造物）の指定）]（文化庁報道発表、2023年11月24日）。令和6年1月19日文部科学省告示第2号。。 * 絵馬 8枚（東松山市指定文化財） 「関羽と張飛」「馬上の中国武人」「武人と騎馬武者」「予譲の仇討」「牛若丸と弁慶」「俵藤太秀郷」「文人脇息に寄り」「○呉服店」（○は○に二） ==牡丹園== [[ファイル:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|牡丹園（2010年5月）]] 箭弓稲荷神社の境内にある[[ボタン (植物)|牡丹]]園は[[1923年]]（大正12年）、[[東武東上本線|東上本線]]坂戸町駅～武州松山駅（現在の東松山駅）間の延伸開業の際に、[[東武鉄道]][[社長]][[根津嘉一郎 (初代)|根津嘉一郎]]から奉納されたものである。現在では、3,500[[平方メートル]]の敷地に1,300株の牡丹がある。 なお、東松山市内には、30,700平方メートルの敷地に9,100株の牡丹がある[[東松山ぼたん園]]もある。 ファイル:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|牡丹園の牡丹 ファイル:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|牡丹園の牡丹とつつじ ==その他== * かつて[[東松山駅]]東口に大鳥居があったが、[[2008年]]（平成20年）秋に駅前の再開発事業により撤去された。なお、この鳥居は箭弓稲荷神社と直接の関係は無かった（東松山市観光協会が建てたものである）。また、東上線の線路を越えた神明町の旧表参道跡にも箭弓稲荷神社の鳥居がある。 * 商売、勝負の神様として特に有名である。 * 「やきゅう」という音との縁で、[[プロ野球]]をはじめとする野球関係者が多く参拝する事でも知られている。特に、同じ埼玉県内の[[所沢市]]に本拠地を構える[[埼玉西武ライオンズ]]の選手が頻繁に訪れる。また、これに因んで「バット[[絵馬]]」「ベース絵馬」等が頒布されている。 * 箭弓稲荷神社周辺の地名は「箭弓町（やきゅうちょう）」といい、同神社に由来している。 * 主な芸能人の崇敬者には落語家[[林家三平 (初代)|初代林家三平]]並びにその子息の[[林家正蔵 (9代目)|9代目林家正蔵]]がいる。[[1964年]]（昭和39年）の[[週刊新潮]]に載る初代林家三平の談によると、海老名家（林家）には代々手のひらに乗るほどの小さな「武州松山の箭弓稲荷様」が伝わっている。いつごろのものかわからないが、海老名家の先祖は講談で有名な町奴、[[幡随院長兵衛]]に登場する旗本・[[水野成之]]（十郎左衛門）の配下である海老名弾正とされ、箭弓稲荷様は海老名家の守り神様で、いつも肌身はなさず持っている。楽屋にいる時は、楽屋に祀り、手を合わせて拝む。この手が拝むときに上がれば、その日の高座の芸はうまくいき、下がれば調子が良くない。誠に霊験あらたかな神様である、という話である。 == 住所 == 埼玉県東松山市箭弓町2-5-14 == アクセス == === 鉄道 === * [[東武東上本線]] [[東松山駅]]西口から徒歩3分 === 自動車 === * [[関越自動車道]] [[東松山インターチェンジ]]から約1.5km == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』神社新報社、1968年、61頁下段 * 白井永二・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、334頁下段 * {{Cite|和書|editor=清水宣弘|title=町場の民俗|publisher=東松山市|series=東松山市史編さん調査報告第22集|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{cite book|和書|title=新編武蔵風土記稿|volume=巻ノ195比企郡ノ10|chapter=松山町|id={{NDLJP|764006/101}}|publisher=内務省地理局|date=1884-6|ref={{sfnref|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[神道]] * [[神社一覧]] * [[美しい日本の歩きたくなるみち500選]] ==外部リンク== * [http://www.yakyu-inari.jp/ 箭弓稲荷神社]（公式サイト） * {{Twitter|yakyuinari0921}} * {{Instagram|yakyuinari}} * {{Facebook|箭弓稲荷神社-683229961783950}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:やきゆういなりしんしや}} [[Category:埼玉県の神社]] [[Category:東松山市の建築物]] [[Category:東松山市の歴史]] [[Category:埼玉県の旧県社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:稲荷神社]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]] [[Category:埼玉県の重要文化財]] revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] to [[Yakyu Inari Shrine]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{redirect|箭弓稲荷神社|[[東京都]][[台東区]]にある当社の[[分霊]]である箭弓稲荷神社|箭弓稲荷神社 (台東区)}} {{参照方法|date=2013年8月}} {{神社 |名称=箭弓稲荷神社 |画像=[[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
鳥居と拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |所在地=[[埼玉県]][[東松山市]]箭弓町2-5-14 |ISO = JP-11 |緯度度 = 36 |緯度分 = 02 |緯度秒 = 04 |経度度 = 139 |経度分 = 23 |経度秒 = 55 |祭神=[[保食神]]
([[宇迦之御魂神]]・[[トヨウケビメ|豊受比賣神]]) |社格=旧[[県社]]・[[別表神社]] |創建=[[712年]]（和銅5年） |本殿=[[権現造]] |別名=やきゅうさま |例祭=[[9月21日]] |神事= |地図 = Japan Saitama }} '''箭弓稲荷神社'''（やきゅういなりじんじゃ）とは、[[埼玉県]][[東松山市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は県社で、戦後は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]となった。 まれに[[稲荷神#日本三大稲荷|日本三大稲荷]]{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11.箭弓稲荷神社 |newspaper=足立朝日 |publisher=朝日新聞販売店 足立朝日会 |date=2009-11-20 |accessdate=2018-11-16}}{{要出典|date=2018年12月|title=箭弓稲荷神社自体に対し日本三大稲荷の一つである案内とする文献}}や[[稲荷神#日本五大稲荷|日本五大稲荷]]{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=西口店近くの有名な神社です 箭弓稲荷神社 |publisher=[[ピタットハウス]]東松山西口店 |accessdate=2018-12-16 }}{{要出典|date=2018年12月|title=箭弓稲荷神社が日本五大稲荷の一つであるとする文献}}の一つとされることがある。 ==歴史== [[712年]]（和銅5年）の創建と伝えられる。その頃は小さな祠だったようだが、[[1030年]]（長元3年）に[[下総国]]の城主[[平忠常]]の討伐に出かけた[[源頼信]]がこの周辺に一泊した際に、近くにあった野久稲荷神社に詣でて、太刀一振と馬一頭を奉納すると、その夜に白羽の矢のような形をした雲が敵陣の方へ飛んでいくのを目撃し、これは神のお告げだと確信、直ちに敵陣に攻め込んだ頼信は快勝し、立派な社殿を建造し「箭弓稲荷大明神」とたたえたと伝えられる。以来、[[松山城 (武蔵国)|松山城]]主、[[川越城]]主などの庇護を受けた。 社名の由来については、[[1028年]]（長元元年）に[[下総国]]の[[平忠常]]が謀反を企て[[安房国|安房]]・[[上総国|上総]]・[[下総国|下総]]の三カ国を制圧し、大軍をもって[[武蔵国]]に侵攻した（[[平忠常の乱]]）。これを受けて源氏の棟梁[[源満仲]]（多田満仲）の子であり、[[甲斐国]]守を勤める[[源頼信]]は、忠常追討の綸旨を賜り、鎮圧に向かった。しかし、多勢に無勢、武勇の頼信も心を悩ませた。その時、頼信が布陣する松山本陣近くで古い祠を見つけた。問えば、野久原に鎮まる「野久稲荷大明神」で、本地は「[[十一面観音|十一面観世音]]」であるという。 これを聞いた頼信は、野久はすなわち箭弓（矢弓）の意で、武門の守護神であると、大いに奮い立ち、神前に怨敵退散の願書、太刀一振、駿馬一頭を奉納し、ついに忠常を撃退することができた。一説には、白狐に乗った神が弓矢を授けたと伝える。この神恩に報いるため、社殿を再建した。以来、野久稲荷は、箭弓稲荷と号せられるようになった。 同形の物語としては、さかのぼること[[791年]]（延暦10年）[[坂上田村麻呂]]が箭弓稲荷より南西約4kmにある[[正法寺 (東松山市)|岩殿観音]]付近の雪見峠（セメント採掘により峠としては現存しないが、地名として残っており現在の東松山市大字岩殿字雪見峠付近）の悪竜を退治する際に、老人の姿の神が矢を授けたとの言い伝えがある。 一方で関連する寺院として、福聚寺の草創については次のようにある。当社は中世の兵乱により衰微し、野中の小社となり、近くの庵主が神前に一灯を供ずるのみになっていた。[[1617年]]（元和3年）、[[天海|天海僧正]]が[[駿府]]から[[下野国]]へ神輿（二度葬される途中の家康と思われる）を守護し、当地松山宿を通行した折、大雨に見舞われたため、当社の宮守りをしていた庵主の草室に神輿を納めた。すると、忽然と弓箭を携えた翁が示現した。天海僧正が何者かと問えば「人にあらず、稲魂の神使なり、僧正守護する神輿の御先を掃仕して非常を静める役を、この松山野久の地に勤めん」と告げた。これを聞いた僧正は、庵主に翁の御告を伝えて当社の由来を聞き、箭弓稲荷大神を尊崇して社殿を造営した。この折、僧正は訪れた庵を一寺に取り立てて福聚寺という寺号を与え、庵主を別当職に補任した。 『[[新編武蔵風土記稿]]』には「享保中より殊に威徳著しく諸人信仰するもの多し　今の如く市店旅宿門前に並へるは彼頃よりのことなり」とある{{sfn|新編武蔵風土記稿 松山町}}。 [[明治]]初年、[[神仏分離]]により福聚寺は別当から離れることとなった。（福聚寺は今も東松山市本町に存在している（[[東松山市立図書館|市立図書館]]から沼をはさんだ反対側）） ==祭神== *[[保食神]]（宇迦之御魂神・豊受比賣神） ==境内== かつては広大な敷地を持っていたが、駅前に位置している事もあり、[[ホテル]]や[[自動車学校]]、テニスコートなどに代わってしまっている。 *'''本殿'''－[[1715年]]（正徳5年）に地頭・島田弾正の計らいにより建造されたもの。 *'''幣殿'''－[[1811年]]（文化8年）の建造と伝えられる。 *'''拝殿'''－[[1835年]]（天保6年）に領主 松平大和守の造営。 *'''穴宮稲荷（団十郎稲荷）'''　- [[市川團十郎_(7代目)|7代目市川団十郎]]は、芸道一筋に生きた歌舞伎役者で当社の熱心な崇敬者であったと伝える。社伝によると、歌舞伎の演じ物に「[[葛の葉]]」「[[義経千本桜|狐忠信]](義経千本桜四段目 河連法眼館の段)」があり、狐の仕草が非常に難しいとされるが、団十郎は稲荷大神に心願し、その加護を受け、ついに江戸柳盛座で見事に演じきり、大盛況のうちに興行を終えることができたという。そのため、大願成就のための石祠一社を[[1821年]]（文政4年）に奉納している{{sfn|清水宣弘|1981|pp=54-59}}。これは、お穴様と呼ばれる穴宮神社で、現在でも技芸向上に励む人々から信仰されている。 8枚（東松山市指定文化財） 「関羽と張飛」「馬上の中国武人」「武人と騎馬武者」「予譲の仇討」「牛若丸と弁慶」「俵藤太秀郷」「文人脇息に寄り」「○呉服店」（○は○に二） ==牡丹園== [[ファイル:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|牡丹園（2010年5月）]] 箭弓稲荷神社の境内にある[[ボタン (植物)|牡丹]]園は[[1923年]]（大正12年）、[[東武東上本線|東上本線]]坂戸町駅～武州松山駅（現在の東松山駅）間の延伸開業の際に、[[東武鉄道]][[社長]][[根津嘉一郎 (初代)|根津嘉一郎]]から奉納されたものである。現在では、3,500[[平方メートル]]の敷地に1,300株の牡丹がある。 なお、東松山市内には、30,700平方メートルの敷地に9,100株の牡丹がある[[東松山ぼたん園]]もある。 ファイル:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|牡丹園の牡丹 ファイル:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|牡丹園の牡丹とつつじ ==その他== * かつて[[東松山駅]]東口に大鳥居があったが、[[2008年]]（平成20年）秋に駅前の再開発事業により撤去された。なお、この鳥居は箭弓稲荷神社と直接の関係は無かった（東松山市観光協会が建てたものである）。また、東上線の線路を越えた神明町の旧表参道跡にも箭弓稲荷神社の鳥居がある。 * 商売、勝負の神様として特に有名である。 * 「やきゅう」という音との縁で、[[プロ野球]]をはじめとする野球関係者が多く参拝する事でも知られている。特に、同じ埼玉県内の[[所沢市]]に本拠地を構える[[埼玉西武ライオンズ]]の選手が頻繁に訪れる。また、これに因んで「バット[[絵馬]]」「ベース絵馬」等が頒布されている。 * 箭弓稲荷神社周辺の地名は「箭弓町（やきゅうちょう）」といい、同神社に由来している。 * 主な芸能人の崇敬者には落語家[[林家三平 (初代)|初代林家三平]]並びにその子息の[[林家正蔵 (9代目)|9代目林家正蔵]]がいる。[[1964年]]（昭和39年）の[[週刊新潮]]に載る初代林家三平の談によると、海老名家（林家）には代々手のひらに乗るほどの小さな「武州松山の箭弓稲荷様」が伝わっている。いつごろのものかわからないが、海老名家の先祖は講談で有名な町奴、[[幡随院長兵衛]]に登場する旗本・[[水野成之]]（十郎左衛門）の配下である海老名弾正とされ、箭弓稲荷様は海老名家の守り神様で、いつも肌身はなさず持っている。楽屋にいる時は、楽屋に祀り、手を合わせて拝む。この手が拝むときに上がれば、その日の高座の芸はうまくいき、下がれば調子が良くない。誠に霊験あらたかな神様である、という話である。 == 住所 == 埼玉県東松山市箭弓町2-5-14 == アクセス == === 鉄道 === * [[東武東上本線]] [[東松山駅]]西口から徒歩3分 === 自動車 === * [[関越自動車道]] [[東松山インターチェンジ]]から約1.5km == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in {{ill|Taito City|ja|台東区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Cite sources|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = [[File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Haiden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{Coord|36|2|4|N|139|23|55|E}}}} |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[Toyoukehime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} set out to subdue {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the lord of {{ill|Shimousa Province|ja|下総国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)|ja|松山城 (武蔵国)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|ja|川越城|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of {{ill|Taira no Tadatsune|ja|平忠常|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼信|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when {{ill|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|ja|坂上田村麻呂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk {{ill|Tenkai|ja|天海|qq=draft|qq=draft}} took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|宇迦之御魂神|1=Ukanomitama|2=ja|3=ウカノミタマ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - {{ill|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|ja|市川團十郎 (7代目)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by {{ill|Nezu Kaichirō|ja|根津嘉一郎 (初代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, president of {{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the {{ill|Saitama Seibu Lions|ja|埼玉西武ライオンズ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer {{ill|Hayashiya Sanpei I|ja|林家三平 (初代)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his son, {{ill|Hayashiya Shōzō IX|ja|林家正蔵 (9代目)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|ja|東松山市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Station|ja|東松山駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Tobu Tojo Line|ja|東武東上本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Kan-Etsu Expressway|ja|関越自動車道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section *
