The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Interchange|1=Higashimatsuyama interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11103558}} on the [[:en:Kan-etsu [[sn:commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603300]] Wikidata name: Yakyū Inari-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in [[:en:Taitō|Taito City]], Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]]
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[:en:September 21|September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama |location = {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|WD=Q823907}} |coordinates = {{Coord|36.034444444444446|N|139.3986111111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268}} }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/箭弓稲荷神社|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)|1=Yakyū Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603300}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] set out to subdue [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]], the lord of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimousa Province]], he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of [[:en:Matsuyama Castle (Ranzan)|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)]] and [[:en:Kawagoe Castle|Kawagoe Castle]]. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama [[sn:commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603300]] Wikidata name: Yakyū Inari-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in [[:en:Taitō|Taito City]], Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]]
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[:en:September 21|September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama |location = {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|WD=Q823907}} |coordinates = {{Coord|36.034444444444446|N|139.3986111111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268}} }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/箭弓稲荷神社|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)|1=Yakyū Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603300}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] set out to subdue [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]], the lord of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimousa Province]], he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of [[:en:Matsuyama Castle (Ranzan)|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)]] and [[:en:Kawagoe Castle|Kawagoe Castle]]. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Interchange|1=Higashimatsuyama interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11103558}} on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 [[commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603300]] Wikidata name: Yakyū Inari-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in [[:en:Taitō|Taito City]], Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]]
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[:en:September 21|September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama |location = {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|WD=Q823907}} |coordinates = {{Coord|36.034444444444446|N|139.3986111111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268}} }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/箭弓稲荷神社|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)|1=Yakyū Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603300}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] set out to subdue [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]], the lord of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimousa Province]], he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of [[:en:Matsuyama Castle (Ranzan)|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)]] and [[:en:Kawagoe Castle|Kawagoe Castle]]. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Interchange|1=Higashimatsuyama interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11103558}} on the [[:en:Kan-etsu [[commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] [[commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Yakyu Inari-jinja]][[ja:箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603300]] Wikidata name: Yakyū Inari-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Redirect|Yakyu Inari Shrine|Yakyu Inari Shrine, a branch in [[:en:Taitō|Taito City]], Tokyo|Yakyu Inari Shrine (Taito City)}} {{Unreferenced|date=August 2013}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Yakyu Inari Shrine |image = File:Yakyuu inari shrine.jpg |location = 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] |ISO = JP-11 |Rank = 36|2|4 |longitude = 139|23|55 |deity = [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]]
([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[:en:September 21|September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama |location = {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|WD=Q823907}} |coordinates = {{Coord|36.034444444444446|N|139.3986111111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268}} }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/箭弓稲荷神社|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)|1=Yakyū Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603300}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. Yakyu Inari Shrine |newspaper=Adachi Asahi |publisher=Adachi Asahi Newspaper Sales Office |date=November 20, 2009 |accessdate=November 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines}} or one of {{ill|Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本五大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.pitat.com/machipita/report/70786 |title=The famous shrine near the Nishi-guchi store, Yakyu Inari Shrine |publisher=Pitatto House Higashimatsuyama Nishi-guchi Store |accessdate=December 16, 2018}}{{Citation needed|date=December 2018|title=Documentation indicating Yakyu Inari Shrine as one of Japan's Five Great Inari Shrines}}. == History == The shrine is said to have been founded in 712 AD (Wadō 5). At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] set out to subdue [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]], the lord of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimousa Province]], he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of [[:en:Matsuyama Castle (Ranzan)|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)]] and [[:en:Kawagoe Castle|Kawagoe Castle]]. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Interchange|1=Higashimatsuyama interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11103558}} on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Since then, it has been protected by the lords of [[:en:Matsuyama Castle (Ranzan)|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)]] and [[:en:Kawagoe Castle|Kawagoe Castle]]. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 ([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[:en:September 21|September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama |location = {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|WD=Q823907}} |coordinates = {{Coord|36.034444444444446|N|139.3986111111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268}} }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/箭弓稲荷神社|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)|1=Yakyū Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603300}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]. The shrine was formerly a prefectural shrine and is currently listed as a special shrine under the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is sometimes considered one of {{ill|Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja|日本三大稲荷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan's Three Great Inari Shrines|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=稲荷神|1=Inari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q719665}}{{Cite news|url=http://www.adachi-asahi.jp/?p=7975 |title=11. At that time, it was a small shrine, but in 1030 AD (Chōgen 3), when [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] set out to subdue [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]], the lord of [[:en:Shimōsa Province|Shimousa Province]], he visited the nearby Noku Inari Shrine, offering a sword and a horse. That night, he saw a cloud shaped like a white arrow flying toward the enemy camp, which he took as a divine sign. He immediately attacked the enemy camp, achieving a great victory, and built a splendid shrine, praising it as "Yakyu Inari Daimyojin." Since then, it has been protected by the lords of [[:en:Matsuyama Castle (Ranzan)|Matsuyama Castle (Musashi Province)]] and [[:en:Kawagoe Castle|Kawagoe Castle]]. The origin of the shrine's name is related to the rebellion of [[:en:Taira no Tadatsune|Taira no Tadatsune]] in 1028 AD (Chōgen 1). Taira had taken control of three provinces and was advancing on Musashi Province. [[:en:Minamoto no Yorinobu|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]], who was tasked with subduing Taira, discovered an old shrine nearby. Learning it was dedicated to Noku Inari Daimyojin, Yoriyoshi, interpreting "Noku" as "arrow and bow," prayed for victory. He offered a sword, a horse, and a prayer for enemy defeat, eventually succeeding in his mission. In gratitude, he rebuilt the shrine, which became known as Yakyu Inari Shrine. A similar story dates back to 791 AD (Enryaku 10), when [[:en:Sakanoue no Tamuramaro|Sakanoue no Tamuramaro]] was granted an arrow by an elderly god to defeat an evil dragon near {{ill|Iwadono Kannon|ja|正法寺 (東松山市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadono Kannon|1=Shōbō-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545560}}. The shrine declined during the Middle Ages but was revitalized in 1617 AD (Genna 3) when the monk [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]] took shelter at the shrine during heavy rain. A divine revelation led to the reconstruction of the shrine, and the nearby temple Fukujūji was established as its guardian temple. {{ill|Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpen Musashi Fudoki|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}} notes the shrine's increased prominence during the Kyōhō period. Following the separation of Shinto and Buddhism in the early Meiji period, Fukujūji ceased to be the shrine's guardian temple. The temple still exists in Higashimatsuyama today. == deity == * [[:en:Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama]] (Ukanomitama no Mikoto・Toyouke no Okami) == Grounds == The shrine once had a vast estate, but much of it has been replaced by hotels, a driving school, and tennis courts. * '''main hall''' - Built in 1715 AD (Shōtoku 5) under the direction of local governor Shimada Danjō. * '''Heiden''' - Built in 1811 AD (Bunka 8). * '''Haiden''' - Built in 1835 AD (Tenpō 6) by the local lord Matsudaira Yamato-no-kami. * '''Anamiya Inari (Danjūrō Inari)''' - [[:en:Ichikawa Danjūrō VII|Ichikawa Danjūrō VII]] was a devoted follower of the shrine. He dedicated a stone shrine in 1821 AD (Bunsei 4) in gratitude for the god's blessings on his kabuki performances. It is still revered by those seeking to improve their artistic skills. == Cultural Properties == * main hall, Heiden, and Haiden (including one main hall, three building tags, one document) - Designated as Important Cultural Properties by the Japanese government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/93968501.html Cultural Affairs Council Recommendation (Designation of Important Cultural Properties)] (Cultural Affairs Agency Press Release, November 24, 2023).Announced as Important Cultural Properties on January 19, 2024, by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology. * Eight ema (wooden plaques) - Designated as cultural properties by Higashimatsuyama City. == Peony Garden == [[File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|Peony Garden (May 2010)]] The peony garden at Yakyu Inari Shrine was donated in 1923 (Taishō 12) by [[:en:Nezu Kaichirō|Nezu Kaichirō]], president of [[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]], when the Tobu Tojo Line was extended. The garden covers 3,500 square meters and contains 1,300 peony plants. There is also a {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|ja|東松山ぼたん園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Peony Garden|1=Higashimatsuyama Botan-en|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527327}} in the city, covering 30,700 square meters with 9,100 peony plants. File:Higashimatsuyama Peony Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies in the Peony Garden File:Higashimatsuyama Azalea Of Yakyuinari Shrine 1.JPG|Peonies and azaleas in the Peony Garden == Other == * A large torii once stood at the east exit of Higashimatsuyama Station but was removed in the fall of 2008 (Heisei 20) due to redevelopment. Although the torii was not directly associated with the shrine, another torii still stands on the old main approach in Shimmeicho. * The shrine is renowned for business and competition success. * Due to its name "Yakyu," which sounds like "baseball" in Japanese, many baseball players visit the shrine. Players from the [[:en:Saitama Seibu Lions|Saitama Seibu Lions]] frequently visit the shrine, and the shrine offers "bat ema" and "base ema." * The surrounding area is named Yakyu-cho after the shrine. * Famous devotees include the first-generation rakugo performer [[:en:Hayashiya Sanpei I|Hayashiya Sanpei I]] and his son, [[:en:Hayashiya Shōzō IX|Hayashiya Shōzō IX]]. In a 1964 interview, Sanpei mentioned that the Ebina family has a small "Yakyu Inari-sama" that they always carry, which brings them good fortune in their performances. == location == 2-5-14 Yakyu-cho, [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]] == Access == === Train === * 3-minute walk from the west exit of [[:en:Higashi-Matsuyama Station|Higashimatsuyama Station]] on the [[:en:Tōbu Tōjō Line|Tobu Tojo Line]] === Car === * Approximately 1.5 km from {{ill|Higashimatsuyama Interchange|ja|東松山インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashimatsuyama Interchange|1=Higashimatsuyama interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11103558}} on the [[:en:Kan-etsu Expressway|Kan-Etsu Expressway]] == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Asu Morohiko & Yoshihiko Umeda (Editors and Supervisors), "Shinto Dictionary," Jinja Shinposha, 1968, p. 61 lower section * Eiji Shirai & Masanori Toki (Editors), "Shrine Dictionary," Tokyo-do Publishing, 1979, p. 334 lower section * {{Cite|language=Japanese|editor=Shimizu Nobuhiro|title=Town Folk Customs|publisher=Higashimatsuyama City|series=Higashimatsuyama City History Research Report No. 22|year=1981|ref=harv}} * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|title={{ill|New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|ja|新編武蔵風土記稿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Musashi Fudoki Manuscript|1=Shinpen Musashi Fudoki-kō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503943}}|volume=Volume 195, Hiki District ([[Ukanomitama|Ukanomitama no Mikoto]]・[[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke no Okami]]) |Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine・Special Shrine |founded = 712 AD (Wadō 5) |main hall = [[Gongen-zukuri]] |alternate name = Yakyu-sama |festival = [[:en:September 21|September 21]] |Rituals = |map = Japan Saitama |location = {{ill|Higashimatsuyama|WD=Q823907}} |coordinates = {{Coord|36.034444444444446|N|139.3986111111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Ishi-no-ma-zukuri|WD=Q3155268}} }} '''Yakyu Inari Shrine''' ({{ill|箭弓稲荷神社|ja|箭弓稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/箭弓稲荷神社|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=箭弓稲荷神社 (東松山市)|1=Yakyū Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603300}}, Yakyu Inari Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Higashimatsuyama, Saitama|Higashimatsuyama]], [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].
